context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help with my philosophy assignment about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five. I’m pretty confused about how an act consequentialist would handle the trolley-style cases, especially Fuel and Blood.
exchanges.tutor:
Start with the simplest lens: for an act consequentialist, what makes an act right or wrong in a particular case?

Try to state that principle in one sentence, then test it on Fuel and Blood: in each case, what are the options, and which option seems to produce the best overall outcome? If you want, give me your one-sentence principle first, and we’ll pressure-test it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should stay in the tutor role, keep the response brief, and use guided discovery rather than giving conclusions. The student is asking about the core concepts of the assignment, so I will narrow the task, ask a solution-directed question, and invite the student to compare cases using the act consequentialist standard. This helps them build their own argument without me supplying a ready-made answer or wording.

exchanges.student: I think the one-sentence principle is: an act is right if, in that specific situation, it leads to the best overall consequences compared to the alternatives. So for Fuel and Blood, I’m guessing the act consequentialist just compares outcomes and says killing one to save five is right if that produces more total good, but I’m not fully sure how Blood is supposed to differ.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That principle is the right starting point.

Now don’t stay abstract—compare the cases. In Blood, is the one person’s death a side effect, or is that person being used as a means to save the five? And would an act consequentialist care about that distinction if the totals come out the same?

Pick one case and tell me: what are the options, and what outcome ranking would the act consequentialist give them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the core act consequentialist principle well. I should affirm that progress briefly, then push them to distinguish the cases by identifying the mechanism of harm and the available alternatives. To avoid giving the answer directly, I will ask targeted comparative questions that guide them toward whether act consequentialism treats the cases differently and whether that seems plausible.

exchanges.student: Okay, I think in Blood the person is being used more directly as a means, not just as a side effect. But I’m guessing an act consequentialist wouldn’t care about that distinction if the result is still 1 dead instead of 5 dead. So the ranking would just be: save five / one dies is better than do nothing / five die, even if that feels kind of harsh.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—now you’re at the real pressure point.

If act consequentialism treats means and side effects the same, what does it say about the different trolley cases overall? Do your own judgments line up with that, or do you think some cases should come out differently?

Name one case where your intuition resists the act consequentialist result, and say why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the means/side-effect distinction and correctly connected it to act consequentialism’s likely indifference to that distinction. I should now push them toward the next assignment component: whether act consequentialism gets the cases right. To preserve guided discovery, I will ask them to articulate the tension between theory and intuition and compare multiple trolley-style cases without supplying a conclusion.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think my intuition resists the case where someone is used as a means, like pushing a person or maybe Blood if that’s the structure. Act consequentialism seems to say it’s fine whenever it saves more people, but that feels wrong to me because it seems like there should be some moral difference between redirecting a threat and intentionally making someone the thing that gets harmed.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now turn that intuition into a rule.

What general principle could explain why redirecting a threat might be permissible, while intentionally using a person as the tool of rescue is not?

Don’t worry about perfect wording. Just propose the core idea: what feature makes the moral difference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the central tension: aggregate outcomes versus the means/side-effect distinction. I should help them move from intuition to argument by asking them to formulate a candidate principle that explains why some killings are permissible and others are not, without giving them a finished theory or wording. Keep it brief and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: Maybe the core idea is that it’s more acceptable to harm someone as a foreseen side effect than to intentionally use that person as the means of saving others. I’m not sure if that’s basically the doctrine of double effect, but I think that’s the kind of distinction I want.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a promising direction. Now test it instead of just naming it.

Apply your distinction to two cases: one where killing seems permissible and one where it seems impermissible. How would your principle classify each, and does it match your intuition better than act consequentialism does?

Try Fuel and Blood first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at the key distinction and tentatively identified double effect. I should affirm the direction but avoid naming it as the answer in a conclusive way. The next step is to test whether their proposed principle can handle the assignment’s cases and whether it improves on act consequentialism. I will ask them to apply it to concrete cases and assess its strengths and limits.

exchanges.student: I think my principle would say Fuel is more likely permissible if the one person’s death is a side effect of redirecting the threat, while Blood is impermissible if the person has to be used as the means to save the five. That matches my intuition better than act consequentialism, because act consequentialism flattens both cases into the same math and I don’t think they feel morally the same.
exchanges.tutor:
Strong progress. But right now you have an intuition plus a distinction—you still need a defense.

Why should “using a person as a means” matter morally? What value is being protected there: rights, inviolability, respect for persons, something else?

Pick one and explain how it helps justify your general account.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a substantive comparative claim. I should now guide them toward strengthening the philosophical argument by asking for justification beyond intuition. This advances the assignment’s requirement to assess whether act consequentialism gets the cases right and to offer a better general account. I will ask a focused why-question and invite them to identify what value their principle protects.

exchanges.student: I think rights or maybe respect for persons makes the most sense. My rough thought is that when someone is used as a means, they’re being treated like a tool instead of as a person with their own moral status, and that seems like a violation that shouldn’t be justified just by adding up benefits to others.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That gives you the backbone of the paper.

Now organize it: what are your 3 main moves?
1) what act consequentialism says,
2) why that seems wrong in some cases,
3) your better general account.

Give me a 1-line summary of each part in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has the core argumentative materials: act consequentialism, objection, and alternative principle grounded in rights/respect. I should prompt them to assemble these into the structure of the assignment rather than adding new concepts. To avoid doing the writing for them, I will ask for a concise outline in their own words.

exchanges.student: Okay, I think part 1 is: act consequentialism says the right act is whichever produces the best overall outcome, so it would allow killing one to save five in both Fuel and Blood if the totals are better. Part 2 is: that seems wrong in cases where the one person is used as a means, because my intuition is that those cases are morally worse than cases where death is just a side effect. Part 3 is: a better account is that killing one to save five is only okay when the person is not intentionally used as a tool, which fits better with rights or respect for persons.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid structure. One weakness: part 3 may be too quick.

How would your view handle a hard case where harming one as a side effect still feels manipulated or unfair? In other words, what limit keeps your principle from being too broad?

Try to state one constraint your view would add.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has assembled a solid three-part structure. I should acknowledge progress, but continue using guided discovery by pressing on a likely weakness: the student is leaning heavily on intuition and needs to clarify scope and handle an objection. I will ask a focused question that moves directly toward a stronger thesis without supplying phrasing or content for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe I need a constraint that it’s not enough for the death to be a side effect; the harm also can’t be deliberately imposed on an innocent person just because they’re available. I’m still fuzzy on how to word that, but I think the idea is that redirecting a threat can be different from selecting someone to bear the cost.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a real improvement. Keep the idea, but you must phrase it yourself.

Test your distinction like this: what is the moral difference between redirecting an existing threat and choosing an innocent person to absorb the harm? If you can explain that clearly in 2–3 sentences, you’ll have your concluding claim.

Write that explanation here in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close to a workable position and is asking about wording, but the rules forbid me from drafting language for them. I should keep them on-task, affirm the conceptual advance, and ask them to articulate the distinction in their own terms with a concrete case test. That preserves Socratic guidance and avoids writing the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: I think the difference is that redirecting an existing threat doesn’t make the innocent person into the object chosen for sacrifice in the same way; their death is a tragic result of stopping a greater harm. But choosing an innocent person to absorb the harm treats that person as if they can be assigned the cost for everyone else’s benefit, which violates their rights and fails to respect them as an individual.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s a defensible core argument.

Main strength: you now explain why act consequentialism seems too crude in Blood-type cases. Remaining task: make sure you explicitly say whether your view rejects act consequentialism altogether or just says it misses an important moral constraint.

Now stop circling and turn this into a full draft in your own words. If you want, paste your intro or first paragraph next.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed a coherent position with a distinction, a criticism of act consequentialism, and a rights/respect-based alternative. I should not write the assignment for them or polish their prose. Following the rules, I will give a brief evaluative response without grading, note the main strength and one remaining task, and push them to integrate what we discussed into a full draft attempt since the conversation has now circled around the same core idea several times.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly. There is no moment where the tutor articulates that this Socratic approach supports learning, retention, or skill development. The rationale for the tutoring method is entirely absent.

After the student successfully articulates a defensible concluding claim in Turn 10 following earlier fuzzy attempts, the tutor moves on to next steps without prompting the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why the improved formulation worked better.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic technique throughout, consistently prompting the student to generate ideas, test principles, and refine their reasoning without giving away answers. Scaffolding is well-executed, building progressively from a basic consequentialist principle to a nuanced rights-based constraint. The main weaknesses are the complete absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach (1.3.A.a) and a missed opportunity to prompt reflection after the student's successful articulation in Turn 10 (1.3.C.a). No other rubric criteria were triggered.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets the student generate key ideas before confirming or redirecting. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on each student contribution and uses targeted questions rather than method dumps. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutor never explains why it is using a question-led approach (1.3.A.a), and after the student's improved formulation in Turn 10, the tutor moves directly to next steps without asking the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking (1.3.C.a). Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: responses are concise, well-anchored to the assignment, and maintain a supportive, non-evaluative tone throughout.
